text:
Narrative 1 - 'While executing a visual approach to Runway 3; we had a EGPWS announcement following a guidance to clear the announcement and continuing visually to the airport and land.'
Narrative 2 - 'Received terrain pull-up message" while level at 2;600 on right base Runway 3 in CHO. Pilot Flying (PF) disconnected Autopilot; climbed; and message was removed. PF continued approach without incident. Suggestion: Do not overfly antennas on right base Runway 3."'
rationale: The flight crew overflew terrain antennas on the base leg without maintaining proper vertical clearance, triggering an EGPWS terrain warning. The incident was a result of human error during visual maneuvering and situational awareness. It was not due to an unsafe or flawed procedure.

text: Narrative 1 - 'Prior to ZZZ Tower opening; Aircraft X called on my approach control freq.; XXX.X; to collect his IFR clearance which I provided. Shortly there after the Pilot called looking for his IFR release and stated he was coming off Runway XX. I called ZZZ1 Tower to make them aware of the traffic; due to the close proximity of the airports and released Aircraft X on a 270 degree heading due to traffic departing ZZZ1 Runway XY on a 340 degree hdg. Shortly there after ZZZ1 Tower called to verify what runway Aircraft X was coming off of and reminded me that IFR departure off Runway XX was NA due to obstructions. I called Aircraft X to see if he was rolling and the pilot said he was so it was too late to stop him. I didn't state 'released Runway XX' since; at that point; it was a non tower controlled airport.The restriction is noted but you have to find it under the ZZZ tab by selecting the AFD tab. This is something that just has to be known and readily recallable when this happens; which is rarely.'
rationale: The controller released an IFR aircraft from a specific runway despite published restrictions prohibiting IFR departures from that runway due to obstructions. The systemic presentation of this restriction required controllers to search deeply within the AFD tab, making it easily missed. This highlights a procedural and systemic flaw in how critical restrictions are documented and presented to controllers.

text: Narrative 1 - 'Completed a series of high performance maneuvers: Steep turns; Power On stalls; Power Off stalls; and Slow flight. My instructor asked me to perform a Steep spiral; which I proceeded to perform as per usual; except that today we sequenced the maneuvers in a different order than normal. I started my steep spiral way lower. I don't remember my starting altitude; but I vocalized the process of the maneuver from memory. The last time we did this maneuver; it was after doing a Lazy Eight; so around 5000 ft MSL. This time; my starting point was at the altitude where I finished my slow flight maneuvers which was preceded by a Power Off stall. I did not realize that I was as low as we were; being in the middle of nowhere. Yet; I picked an intersection to do my steep spiral that was just in the kiddy corner of a newly built cluster of homes; perhaps a half a dozen to a dozen homes. When I finished my second turn I was at 3100 feet. I did not like the closeness of the ground so cancelled the maneuver and with full power; climbed away. We were in a fairly sparsely populated area except for the small cluster of newly built homes. In any case; I thought we were at least 500-600 feet above ground since the airport less than 10 miles away was at 2500 ft MSL; but my instructor looked it up on his ForFlight and discovered that the ground there was at 2800 ft; not 2500 ft; so we were 300 feet above ground. No wonder; I cancelled that maneuver. In reviewing this maneuver; I made the following observations: (1)	I should have used a maneuver checklist to ensure that both the starting altitude and the floor of the maneuver would have been identified before starting the maneuver and followed. This maneuver starts at 4500 ft AGL and the floor needs to be no lower than 1500 ft AGL.(2)	When my guts were telling me: 'don't complete your turn; you are too low.' I should have cancelled the turn and start my climb; right then and there. We would have been above the 500 feet limit; and it would have been much safer; should an emergency occur.(3)	When I started doing these maneuvers; I reviewed these steps very religiously; but after awhile of not doing this maneuver; I stopped looking at it.(4) I am training to be a CFI and we were starting to transition the role of instructor to me; which added a bit of load on my mind; having to verbalize everything and doing maneuvers in different sequences. Things happen fast and relying solely on memory makes you more prone to inevitable distractions; such as mine today.Lessons learned: (1) I put the high performance maneuver checklist back in my every-day bundle to have it with me on each flight. (2) When I plan a training flight with high performance maneuvers; I will review all of them prior to departing so it is fresh on my mind. Safety first. A co-worker of mine works in that area; so I asked her if she saw or heard us. She usually is outside with her little boy; but she said no.'
rationale: The student pilot initiated a steep spiral at an inappropriately low altitude, leading to the maneuver being performed dangerously close to the ground. This occurred due to a human failure to mentally sequence the maneuvers, verify the starting altitude, and use the required checklist. The primary problem was cognitive distraction and loss of situational awareness, not a flawed training procedure.

text: Narrative 1 - 'On the BACHR1 STAR to Dallas Love field. Fort Worth Center told us to descend and maintain 24;000 feet. We complied and leveled off at our assigned altitude of FL240. Shortly before the ANGST fix we were given descend via the arrival. I then set the lowest altitude on the BACHR1 (RNAV) STAR IN the Altitude select and activated the VNAV I then slowed to the crossing speed restriction of 280kts @FL240. My attention was diverted for no more that a minute. When I saw we had started our descent prematurely and passing below our hard alt of FL240 AT ANGST I disengage the autopilot and stopped the descent. The autopilot and FMS were reset and after that all other fixes were crossed at the appropriate altitudes... (THERE WERE NO TRAFFIC CONFLICTS)..After landing we as a crew discussed what could have been done to prevent this and keep it from ever happening again.'
rationale: The flight crew experienced an automation anomaly where the VNAV system initiated a premature descent below their assigned hard altitude. Despite following the proper steps to set up the descent via clearance, the aircraft's automation logic behaved unexpectedly. This points to a procedural or systemic issue with the FMS rather than a simple manual flying mistake.

text:
Narrative 1 - 'Enroute to DEN ATC changed our arrival three times. Expecting the ILS to Runway 16R ATC issued a last minute clearance for the RNAV RNP Z to 16R. This occurred during the descent and transition from DEN Center to Denver Approach. We received a 'descend via' clearance from Center with an 11;000 ft level off altitude. We checked in with Approach Control passing through approximately 14;000 ft. This is when we received the approach change. Our focus at this point was to load and brief the approach. Approach issued a speed restriction and an altitude restriction of 13;000 feet. We were in VNAV PATH still on the arrival approaching the transition fix (CLIFF) for the approach. Due to task saturation we missed the altitude restriction. Passing 12;500 feet; Approach asked what our assigned altitude was and we replied 11;000 feet. The controller stated 13;000 feet was assigned and re-cleared us to descend to 11;000 feet. DEN is the worst airport in our system when it comes to late changes to arrivals; approaches and runway assignments. There needs to be better coordination between Center; Approach and Tower.'
Narrative 2 - 'We were on the arrival phase of our flight to DEN. The Captain was the Pilot Flying and I was the Pilot Monitoring. As we approached DEN arrival airspace; our STAR was changed due to TRWs (thunderstorms) west and overhead the DEN Airport. We were handed off to DEN Approach and were given a different STAR along with TRW avoidance vectors. We were then given a couple of orbits as that STAR was unflyable due to TRWs. We were then given vectors and told to set up for our third STAR into DEN of which only a small segment was flown due to TRW deviations. The STAR we eventually flew the last segment of was the PEEKE 3 landing 16R. During the arrival phase we were subjected to continuous light to moderate turbulence with similar sounding call sign aircraft. The frequency was very congested with multiple deviating aircraft. To say the environment was in the Yellow would be an understatement. Our clearance surrounding this event was to slow to 210 knots and maintain 13;000 ft; I read this back and the Captain verbally verified this. I; the Pilot Monitoring; looked to my EFB to make the third arrival change to Runway 16R RNP Z and to begin cross checking my approach setup. When I looked back at the altimeter tape I noticed that we were descending and simultaneously DEN Approach asked for our altitude verification. I responded at 12;700 ft and the Controller stated that we were cleared to maintain 13;000 ft but now to continue to 11;000 ft. The Captain questioned me about descending to 11;000 ft and I told him that we had been cleared to maintain 13;000 feet. There was no traffic conflict with this event and the flight continued to 16R with normal operations.My solution to this event is based upon 29 years of part 121 operations and experience as a pilot with four different airlines which ranges from a regional airline where I was a check airman to worldwide operations where I served as an L-1011 First Officer and B-757 relief Captain; and to previously serving as a B-737 Captain. I have never been more task saturated nor seen a Pilot Flying position more task saturated than the way Company Flight Ops defines its roles for the Pilot Flying and Pilot Monitoring. It is clearly set upon the historical foundation of a single pilot approach to flying the aircraft. I witness these gross vertical and lateral deviations virtually every single trip that I fly at Company but 99.9% of the time; the errors are caught before a deviation takes place. In the name of BEST industry practices; I have never been a part of a Flight Ops procedure where the Pilot Flying was heads down making route changes while flying the aircraft; especially during the busiest flight segment which is the approach environment; meanwhile; a perfectly capable Pilot Monitoring is sitting there doing nothing but looking. The other fundamental issue that plays in to this event on a daily basis is the fact that we are focused on the MCP; which is nothing more than a dumb keyboard; verses looking at and literally READING what the FMA is telling us what the ACTUAL mode the aircraft is in. This is such a serious problem within the aviation world that the corporate jet avionics manufacturers have stopped putting annunciator lights on the MCP thus FORCING the pilots to read the FMA; which is the gospel for the vertical and lateral modes. The worst case procedure within our industry is to have the Pilot Monitoring make all of the FMC changes when the aircraft is below 10;000 feet. The best and simplest example of this approach of allowing the Pilot Flying to have the least amount of task saturation is to have the Pilot Monitoring make ALL FMC route changes from takeoff until landing and the Pilot Flying merely has to verify and sit back and maintain the biggest picture of situational awareness available. The other factor involved in this event is that we do not read from the FMA as the primary mode indicator for the verticals and lateral mode the aircraft is in. It is merely a secondary annunciation placing the MCP as the priority mode annunciation. It is a routine observation to see a Captain make a mode change and only look at the MCP and state what he sees and thinks what the mode is only to have me point to the FMA and point out that the aircraft is NOT in the mode that he stated that it was. This is such a simple fundamental fix. Boeing spent millions on Research and Development and FAA approval with all of the labeling inside the cockpit; all we have to do is read it and not make up our own words. This observation is based upon 4 different airlines and is shared by the overwhelming majority of pilots that I have flown with at Company. With safety being job number one; the Pilot Flying should have the least amount of task saturation and distraction while flying the aircraft and the Pilot Monitoring should be the one who is heads down doing the typing. During our day to day operation we must keep the operation simple and READ the FMA and the other captions that Boeing has already labeled for us and reduce the Pilot Flying workload as much as possible to achieve the highest level of situational awareness available.'
rationale: ATC repeatedly changed the arrival and approach clearances during a critical descent phase, leading to task saturation for the flight crew. The late and confusing systemic coordination between Center, Approach, and Tower created an inherently overwhelming environment. The primary problem stems from flawed ATC operational procedures and complex FMS automation logic during late changes.

text:
Narrative 1 - 'We had a stable approach no issues (Runway XX). When we were about to touchdown we got blown to the left of center line. Upon touching down; the plane yawed a-little left; at that point the pilot added full power and attempted a go around. The aircraft did not climb very well at this point. We did get off the ground to a degree. We were getting blown to the left. When we made it to the taxiway and landed the aircraft on the taxiway. This was the best decision as we still had not started to climb. There was no damage to the plane or facility.Person A and I talked about what had happen and what could have been done differently.  1. Choose another airport without 12-15 kt direct cross wind. Crab a-little longer to stay centered on runway.2. Practice on crosswind landings!'
Narrative 2 - 'On Day 0; at around XA:23 Zulu time; Aircraft X experienced an incident during landing at ZZZ. I was the pilot in command. In my flight; I was accompanied by my safety pilot.During the approach for Runway XX; the wind reported 040 13G 17( direct crosswind) . During the landing phase; the aircraft was blowing to the left but swiftly applied full power and attempted to go around. Due to the wind constantly pushing the aircraft to the left; the aircraft underperformed during the climb. Hearing the stall sound; I noticed that the aircraft was unable to climb. So; I rapidly lowered the nose and landed on the taxiway. The landing was successful; with no harm to either the aircraft or anyone else.My safety pilot and I talked about the incident that happened; and we learned a lot. We could avoid this issue by landing at a different airport due to the strong direct crosswind at ZZZ Airport.Even while mistakes are inevitable; I think this experience gave me important insights into how to prevent them by being extremely cautious and making better decisions.'
rationale: The pilot failed to adequately compensate for a strong direct crosswind during the landing phase, allowing the aircraft to be blown off the runway centerline. The subsequent decision to attempt a delayed go-around after touchdown resulted in poor climb performance and landing on the taxiway. This was fundamentally a human error in manual aircraft control and aeronautical decision-making.

text:
Narrative 1 - 'Air carrier flight crew reported they were cleared by ATC to cross a runway that had an aircraft on take-off roll. As the flight crew taxied toward the runway hold short line; ATC directed them to stop when they realized another aircraft was rolling. Flight crew stopped aircraft and crossed after other aircraft had departed.After landing [Runway] XXR in ZZZ we cleared rwy and taxied 1 to 2 and were instructed to holdshort of rwy XXL on [taxiway] 1. After a few minutes and multiple departures on XXL we heard that Aircraft Y was cleared for takeoff. Then we heard Aircraft Z was told to line up and wait. Moments later we got a clearance to cross rwy XXL at [taxiway] 1. As I slowly initiated power to cross; I looked left at the aircraft I thought was holding with their lights on. I was trying to determine if it was moving or stopped.  Unbeknownst to us; this was Aircraft Y commencing their take-off roll.  At the same time that I thought this aircraft was possibly rolling; I started to stop our aircraft; it was then that the Tower Controller told us to Aircraft X; Stop; Stop;Stop !" At this point I could tell Aircraft Y definitely was rolling. We went about 20 ft. past the HS line on txwy 1; but still probably 50 ft. short of rwy XXL. Aircraft Y  took off in front of us. After receiving the clearance to cross rwy XXL a second time; neither my copilot or I realized that Aircraft Y had not taken off yet. Only hearing and thinking Aircraft Z was holding in position. I called ATC after block in and talked with Person A; Tower Supervisor. He called backed and notified me that we did nothing wrong and that this would be filed as a Controller error.Cause: Weather at the time was night; rain; visibility 2-3 mi; 800 ft. ovc. Atc twr loss of situational awareness; lack of Runway Status Lights (RWSL); both RHLs & Takeoff Hold Lights (THLs); high operation tempo at ZZZ.Suggestion: The Controllers; crew and gnd radar were the last lines of defense in what could have been a very different outcome."'
Narrative 2 - 'After landing XXR in ZZZ we cleared the runway and were instructed to taxi 1 to 2 and hold short of XXL on [taxiway] 1.  We held for a few minutes for several departures.  Then we heard Aircraft Y cleared for takeoff and Aircraft Z cleared to lineup and wait XXL.  Moments later we got a clearance to cross XXL at 1 and taxi to spot X and contract ramp.  I cleared right and the CA was looking left as he pushed up power to start crossing.  The CA realized the aircraft was rolling as tower controller told us stop.  We had gone about 15 ft. past the hold short line on 1 but were still short of the XXL runway by about 50 ft.  Aircraft Y took off and then we were one again cleared to cross XXL at 1 to ramp.  After receiving our clearance to cross runway XXL the second time neither the CA or I had realized Aircraft Y  had not taken off yet.  We were only thinking that Aircraft Z was holding in position.  The CA called the tower after block in and talked with the tower supervisor.  They notified us that it was controller error as he controller cleared Aircraft Y for takeoff and then cleared us to cross before verifying the runway was clear.  Cause: Wx - night; rain; viz was 2-3 SM; 800 ft. overcast; Lack of Runway Status Lights (RWSL); both RHLs & Takeoff Hold Lights (THLs); high ops tempo at ZZZ. Suggestions: Add status lighting system like RWSLs.'
rationale: The Tower Controller cleared an aircraft to cross an active runway while another aircraft was simultaneously cleared for takeoff on that exact runway. The lack of systemic safety features, such as Runway Status Lights (RWSL), allowed the controller's clearance error to go unchecked. The incident highlights a severe procedural and systemic failure in traffic management logic at a high-tempo airport.

text: Narrative 1 - 'I was tasked with inspecting a pilot discrepancy on the engine nose spinners with missing and delaminating black protection covering. RH engine spinner had a small amount missing about ½ inch in diameter. The LH engine spinner had a very large area missing about a 3 x 12 inch strip at edge by retaining ring. The adhesion of remaining protection material was loose around the torn edges. The loose material was not addressed in the Manufacturer's Continue to Operate Procedures which made it possible for separation and ingestion of the coating into the engine. This is an unsafe condition and what to do with the loose material needs addressed for continued safe operation. There is a service bulletin scheduled to come out on this problem but has not been released. My concern of foreign object damage to the engine by the loose protective coating is what I am trying to resolve. I believe the Manufacturer should require replacement of the protective coating before next flight. A simple loaner pool of spinners for replacement while the original spinner is repaired would be a good solution.'
rationale: The manufacturer's documented Continue to Operate Procedures lacked explicit instructions on how to handle loose or delaminating protective coating on engine nose spinners. This omission in the formal documentation created an unsafe condition where the material could be ingested into the engine. The primary issue was a flaw in the maintenance manuals, not a failure by the technician to follow them.

text: Narrative 1 - 'Cargo Aircraft Only (CAO) label discovered during the loading process.'
rationale: A piece of cargo containing a Cargo Aircraft Only (CAO) label was mistakenly loaded or processed incorrectly. This represents a human failure to properly inspect or adhere to standard loading instructions. The incident resulted from a breakdown in human attention and verification, rather than incorrect shipping procedures.

text: Narrative 1 - 'I was assigned to a gate return for flight Aircraft X ZZZ-ZZZ1. The original Crew assigned to the push back noticed an engine leak and informed the Captain before takeoff. The Ramp/Cargo Manager went out on to the taxiway and inspected both engines before giving the flight deck the thumbs up the plane was clear for takeoff. I watched as the Manager inspected the aircraft without proper training to clear the issue regarding the leak. A Ramp/Cargo Manager went outside of his authority and OKed a plane with an engine leak and informed the Captain the plane was clear for takeoff; however the plane had orders to return to gate for a Maintenance Technician to inspect the issue. Aircraft Maintenance was immediately notified of the event. Removal of the Manager who overstepped his authority on the ramp. Understanding your assignments as a Manager and letting the proper and qualified individuals correct the issue. If safety is our most important goal; such gross violations and disregard for Passenger and Flight Crew safety must be addressed.'
rationale: A ramp manager exceeded his formal authority by inspecting an engine leak and clearing the aircraft for takeoff without proper maintenance training. The systemic operational flow allowed an unqualified manager to bypass established safety protocols and override maintenance requirements. This points to a severe procedural breakdown in organizational authority and dispatch logic.

text: Narrative 1 - 'Passenger in seat X failed to comply with crew member instructions on wearing a facial covering at all times in flight; other than eating or drinking. Passenger created a disturbance around him and made fellow passengers extremely uncomfortable.  All passengers and myself were and are concerned about COVID exposure.'
rationale: A passenger refused to comply with crew member instructions regarding mandatory facial coverings, creating a disturbance in the cabin. The incident was caused solely by the behavioral choices and non-compliance of the individual passenger. It was a human factors issue related to passenger behavior, not a defect in the airline's procedures.

text: Narrative 1 - 'Captain of Aircraft X called reported of a NWS (Nosewheel Steering) INOP caution message after pushback. I walked the Captain through the reset procedures in the FIM. The reset procedure did not work. I had the Captain power the aircraft down; wait five minutes then power the aircraft back up and call me back when he did. The Captain never called back. When he landed; Captain wrote the discrepancy: 'STEERING INOP caution complied with MX procedure; issue resolved.'I think there was miscommunication of procedures and who is responsible to write up the discrepancy or when.I will advise the pilots to have a discrepancy entered before performing any type of reset procedure.'
rationale: The flight crew documented an aircraft discrepancy by stating it was resolved via maintenance procedure without proper verification or communication. The formal reporting process lacked clear logic on who was responsible for writing up the discrepancy and when it should be entered. This highlights a procedural ambiguity in the maintenance reporting workflow rather than a simple human slip.

text:
Narrative 1 - 'At 10;000 ft.; sterile FD; descent into DFW; we began preparation procedures [in the] galley and cabin. Once finished; FA3 (Flight Attendant) asked who was in the forward lavatory and we determined the wheelchair passenger at 1F must have gone to the bathroom. FA3 knocked and told her we were landing now and to return to her seat now. She said; 'Ok; I'm coming.' FA1 & FA3 continued asking her to return to her seat and when the wheels dropped. She was still in the bathroom saying she was coming. FA3 and FA1 continued communicating with her and at less than 1 minute to landing the decision was made that she would not be able to safely make it to a seat and she was informed to 'stay in the lavatory.' The flight landed and taxied to toward Gate A19 at which time FA1 informed the Captain we had a passenger land in the lavatory. The Captain asked for basic information at this time. FA1 informed him that the passenger was still in the lavatory and if we were going to be stopped so she could return to her seat. The Captain stated; 'We're going to keep moving.' The passenger was told to remain in the lavatory until the seatbelt sign was turned off. Once we parked at the gate; the passenger came out on her own and apologized saying she was getting sick. FA1 confirmed her name as Passenger A. The Captain then brought FA1 into the cockpit and stated that he should have known we didn't have a passenger in their seat for landing and that he had to do reports now.'
Narrative 2 - 'After [the] Captain made [the] 'prepare for landing announcement' I; FA3 (Flight Attendant); walked up to [the] front and started clearing the cabin from there to [the] back. I finished preparing the cabin and headed up to my seat. When I was getting ready to sit; I saw that there was someone in front lavatory and asked FA1 if he saw anyone go in there. He explained that passenger 1F who came on [a] wheelchair had to use [the] lavatory last minute. I noticed how close we were getting to the ground so I started knocking on [the] door and told her; 'We are approaching landing and you need to come out;' to which she responded; 'I'm coming;' so I kept knocking on door and telling her she needed to come out already. Then; I noticed the landing gear is coming out. I then tell her; 'You need to come out NOW; landing gear is down we are about to land.' She said; 'Okay;' and seemed like she was going to come out but then didn't. That's when FA1 got up and told tell her to stay in the lavatory; which at that point; [was] so close to [the] ground and [since there was] such little time; [it] seemed like the best decision.We landed safely and checked on [the] passenger; she said she was okay. FA1 called [the] flight deck and explained to the Captain that we had someone in the lavatory [and] to let us know if we were going to make any stops so that we could have her go back to [her] seat safely since it takes her time to walk to her seat. The Captain told FA1; 'We are going to keep moving;' so [the] passenger remained in [the] lavatory until we came to a safe stop. The passenger came out and apologized for what she had done and said she understood she had done wrong but she really needed to go. Captain asked us why we didn't call him in the air we explained everything happened just so suddenly and we were so close to [the] ground with sterile flight deck and basically not much time.'
rationale: A passenger unexpectedly entered the lavatory during the critical descent phase and failed to exit despite repeated instructions from the flight attendants. The crew's decision-making and communication were compromised by time pressure as the aircraft rapidly approached landing. The root cause was human behavior, distraction, and the passenger's physical state, not a flaw in the cabin securement procedures.

text: Narrative 1 - 'There is no social distancing being enforced at ZZZ lobby and people are at risk of contracting COVID-19 and most importantly our employees are being exposed day in and day out. The company is not doing enough to protect us from contracting this virus.'
rationale: Employees and passengers experienced a lack of enforced social distancing in the airport lobby, leading to concerns about COVID-19 exposure. This represents a human failure by staff and individuals to physically maintain safe distances as required. The issue stems from human behavior and crowd management, rather than the absence of documented health guidelines.

text:
Narrative 1 - 'Sat on transportation bus for approximately one hour for ground stop blue light (thunderstorms overhead) conditions at ZZZZ after processing through immigration.  Arrived at aircraft and stood underneath nose in the rain for approximately 20 minutes.  Accomplished walk around while Captain continued to wait underneath the nose of the aircraft until we were allowed on the aircraft.  Paperwork delivered to aircraft approximately 30 minutes after boarding aircraft.  During pre-flight compared previously printed ACARS fuel data with actual ACARS.  Captain entered A1 for fuel type and asked for confirmation.  I stated no FSF (Fuel Service Form) yet.  We were waiting for new release form at which time Jumpseater opened chiller door at the same time ground crew was closing Door X due to heavy rain.  Chiller door contacted Door X causing minor damage to Door X.  We annotated the damage in the AML and notified maintenance.  Subsequently; new release form with sign off; DG paperwork; and final W&B (Weight and Balance) all arrived at the same time.  Completed review of new paperwork then ran before engine start checklist and confirmed W&B and fuel computations.  After uneventful flight and landing with 23.0 FOB; ACARS would not close.  No response on SATCOM or cell phone so Skyped flight office from hotel at which time we realized maintenance never provided FSF.Significant delays caused by COVID screening; weather; and transportation issues combined with distractions caused by Door X damage and late paperwork delivery resulted in overlooking the missing FSF.Slow down procedures and verification of required paperwork regardless of perceived time constraints and other distractions.'
Narrative 2 - 'In Country X taking over an hour to go through the COVID-19 process we got on the terminal bus to go to the aircraft across the field. While on the bus; thunderstorms created a blue light (thunderstorms) conditions and the bus was stopped for over one hour. After the conduction subsided the bus continued to the aircraft. Once the air stairs were against the aircraft we got off the bus to board the aircraft. Ramp personnel stopped us and we had to stand under the nose while it was raining for approximately 20 minutes (the bus had left). During normal preflight I ask of fuel type and FO stated we hadn't gotten a fuel ticket yet. I entered A1 in MCDU for fuel from historical knowledge. While waiting for a new release form; a jumpseater has opened the chiller door. Door X coming down hit the chiller door and did some slight damage. We made a write up [in] AML about the cooler door and called maintenance. We received DG paperwork; a new release form with sign off; and final all at the same time. Completing these tasks just before push we cleared the aircraft of ground personnel and closed the doors. Ran before engine start checklist and confirmed weight and balance and fuel computations. After an uneventful flight and landing with 23.0 FOB ACARS would not close the flight out. After calling flight coordination; we realized ZZZZ maintenance never gave us a fuel service form. Significant delays from COVID screening; weather; and transportation combined with jumpseater and Door X damage and late paperwork resulted in overlooking the missed FSF.Slowing down and following procedures even though having perceived time constraints for being late and other distractions.'
rationale: The flight crew pushed back and departed without receiving the required Fuel Service Form after facing multiple delays, weather events, and minor aircraft damage. The accumulation of these events caused task saturation and cognitive distraction, leading the crew to simply forget to verify the paperwork. The incident was a result of human error and time pressure, not a flaw in the dispatch documentation procedures.

text: Narrative 1 - 'I've had four go-arounds in the last 440 hrs. One in November; one in December; one in January (?); and one in March. Here's my observation; as pilots we want to notify ATC as soon as possible of the G/A. The problem is that ATC immediately likes to give us turns and altitudes. The problem from a pilot's perspective is that it's much busier than just after takeoff and ATC normally just leaves us alone on during a takeoff. Yes; I understand the need to ensure separation *if* it's an urgent need; but if it's not an urgent need; how about allowing the a/c on the G/A to at least get the gear doors closed; count to 10 potato and then have ATC issue heading and altitudes?'
rationale: ATC consistently issues complex heading and altitude changes immediately after an aircraft declares a go-around. This systemic timing of instructions conflicts with the pilot's high workload during a critical phase of flight, preventing them from safely reconfiguring the aircraft. The primary problem is a flawed procedural sequence in how ATC manages standard go-arounds.

text: Narrative 1 - 'Departed DTS with a clearance and VFR squawk from Eglin approach. Instructed to 'remain 1 mile south of the coast until airborne contact with Eglin'. At 400 feet and turning west south of the coast; contacted Eglin and they verified radar contact. Eglin then proceeded to get very busy and gave multiple instructions to multiple civilian and military aircraft; of which it was not possible to decipher which instructions were for whom. As I am flying along at 1000 feet westbound; I determine that Eglin is instructing me to contact Hurlburt tower as I am about to penetrate the boundary of Hurlburt class D while under positive control from Eglin. I am not sure if I established contact with HRT tower before penetrating their class D.  DTS absolutely needs a VFR departure procedure which does not require initial contact with Eglin (such as 'departure 14'; proceed east on 120 heading or west on heading 210 at or below 1200 MSL until contact with Eglin. If unable to establish two-way radio communication proceed...') although DTS is presently constructing a control tower; I believe in the absence of a VFR dep procedure; the tower will only create IFR-like flow control for all traffic regardless of VFR/IFR ops.'
rationale: A VFR aircraft departing an airport without a control tower was required to immediately establish contact with a busy approach facility while penetrating an adjacent Class D airspace. The lack of a published, formal VFR departure procedure created systemic confusion and an inability to safely decipher ATC instructions. The incident highlights a procedural gap in the local airspace design.

text: Narrative 1 - 'My student and I were cleared to take off Runway XX. As we rolled on to the runway and started to take off; I heard Tower cleared Aircraft Y ' to cross over midfield' is all I heard. As we started to climb our electronic system warned us with the repeating ' traffic' 'traffic'. I looked slightly up and to the left of the aircraft and the helicopter was coming straight at us. I grabbed the yolk from my student pushing the aircraft down towards the runway to try and avoid the helicopter. Successfully avoided the helicopter by near margins.'
rationale: A helicopter was cleared to cross midfield directly into the departure path of an aircraft taking off from the active runway. The resulting near midair collision was narrowly avoided by the instructor pilot's evasive action. The incident was caused by a human failure in situational awareness and ATC traffic scanning, not a documented procedure telling the controller to route traffic into conflicts.

text: Narrative 1 - 'During our arrival into LAX; we were given a series of speed changes beginning with LAX Center. First it was slow to 260; then increase to 280; then maintain 280 but comply with the STAR. We were also cleared to descend via the HLYWD1. When we checked on with SoCal Approach; the controller told us to slow to 230 before any further descent. The Pilot Flying (PF) used VS mode to arrest the descent to facilitate the speed reduction and began decreasing the speed. We were at around 500 FPM down when the controller said to stop descending and slow to 210. I initially was a little confused about the clearance - whether it meant to stop at our current altitude or if it meant that we wouldn't receive a further descent beyond the arrival until we slowed. Evidently it was the former; but I was unable to clarify due to frequency congestion. When I was able to ask for more information about our clearance; we were already slowed and level at around 16000; and I asked if we'd missed an instruction at some point; due to the unusual speed request. The controller proceeded to give a long explanation about a new procedure that requires 50 or 60 miles of separation to get an IFR departure out of HHR. This left us very high; and the controller then asked us to expedite to 10000. We were beginning our descent and then the controller gave us another speed change; this time to 300 and to give him the best rate down. The PF was rapidly trying to manipulate the automation modes to comply and the controller kept asking for more descent rate. We were doing something like 3000 FPM down and increasing; when we both realized we were unclear about our further clearance. I inquired about what to do after 10000 and the controller just said to get down as fast as possible. At this point we were still high and fast; but pretty far out. We were changed to the final approach controller and given a speed reduction to 250 and then cleared for the ILS 25L approach. We were then high again; and the PF used a vertical direct-to to command the aircraft to rejoin the descent path. I was looking away momentarily to change my chart from the arrival to the approach when the PF inadvertently activated the change before I could review it. I was concerned that we might miss the crossings at KRAIN; TAROC; and DYMMO but we were very high and I am pretty sure we were well above them. While trying to get back 'on profile' the PF accidentally descended below the VNAV path to catch the ILS glide slope just outside HUNDA. I didn't see it right away because I was monitoring our speed reduction and talking to the tower controller; but as I noticed it I remarked to watch the altitude at HUNDA and on my altimeter we were around 100 feet low. During the debrief the PF said that her altimeter read only about 50 feet low. We talked about the challenges of the arrival and approach and how the altitudes on the approach are mandatory crossing altitudes and are particularly important at LAX due to the airspace below the glideslope. In thinking this over after the fact; I think if I had taken the controls and used Touch Control Steering (TCS) to override the autopilot; I probably could have leveled us in time to not be low at HUNDA. It was so close and there was so little time; that by the time I noticed it and said something to the PF it was too late. A few moments earlier and I could have said something and then made the correction. Taking the controls from a First officer (FO) has always been a last resort action for me; but perhaps I should have been more forward in this instance. Also in retrospect; I could have asked for a heading and an altitude back during the confusing descent instructions; positioned the aircraft in a more obvious condition with a clearer clearance; then rejoined the arrival in a more normal configuration and position.'
rationale: ATC issued a rapid succession of confusing speed and descent clearances that contradicted the published STAR. The systemic lack of coordination and the controller's demand for excessive descent rates forced the flight crew to rapidly manipulate automation modes, leading to an altitude deviation. This was a procedural failure driven by chaotic ATC instructions and complex automation logic.

text: Narrative 1 - 'Upon arrival; a wheel chair was brought plane side for a passenger. It was noted that the wheelchair had an integrated lithium ion battery. No notification was given; and no paperwork was received by us. Arrival station agent believed it to be a manual wheelchair. Departure from ZZZ was time compressed due to an issue with aircraft cleaners and the resulting delay. It would seem that the paperwork was overlooked in part due to the pressures applied to the agents and ramp personnel to secure a departure ASAP.'
rationale: A wheelchair with an integrated lithium-ion battery was loaded without the required notification or paperwork. This omission occurred because the station agents were under extreme time pressure and workload due to prior delays. The primary problem was a human failure to recognize the battery type and follow documentation rules, driven by haste and distraction.
